PixiJS V5 Tutorial (23) - Tweaking Values Live with dat.GUI

In this post we actually wire up dat.GUI so you can tweak things like velocity and alpha live, without editing the code and refreshing every time. We adjust the previous example, map the GUI controls to the right spots, and finish with a quick look at the PixiJS Chrome devTools extension.
JavaScript Core Concepts (21) - Objects - How Objects Are Structured

Time to start the chapters on objects. We'll look at object literals, what a property/value pair actually is, and the other way to create objects using a constructor — plus why that constructor approach has a couple of gotchas you probably want to avoid.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (22) - Debugging Pixi, and Meet dat.GUI

Debugging in Pixi is genuinely painful — there's no DOM to inspect in devTools, so tweaking parameters is a slog of edit, save, refresh, repeat. This post introduces dat.GUI, a tool that gives you a graphical panel to adjust your objects live. We'll get familiar with it here and actually wire it into Pixi in the next post.
JS Core Concepts (20): Operators, Types & Grammar — Practice Quiz

Pretty much every chapter wraps up with a practice quiz, and this one's no different. Ten short JavaScript questions covering typeof, null being an object, new wrappers, loose vs strict equality, object references, operator precedence, pre/post increment, and the classic ASI semicolon traps. Try each one before peeking at the answer.
JavaScript Core Concepts (19): Logical Operators and Faking Default Values Before ES6

Time to talk about logical operators. The thing about && and || in JavaScript is that they don't just return true or false — they return one of the actual operands, and if you don't watch out, the result can surprise you. We'll walk through &&, ||, and !, then use the OR trick to fake a function default value the old-school way, back before ES6 gave us real defaults.
PixiJS V5 Tutorial (21) - Collision Boundaries (3): Random Positions and Centering Text

This one picks up where the last post left off and fixes a few small things, so most of the code carries over from before — which means this post is a bit shorter. We'll randomize where the crit effect spawns using Math.random, and fix the text so it sits dead-center in the star shape.
JavaScript Core (18) — Operators, Types & Grammar: Truthy and Falsy

Truthy and falsy values show up most often inside conditionals — and that's exactly where they hide a bunch of traps. In this post we look at why Truthy/Falsy isn't really the same thing as implicit coercion, how to read it off a truth table, and why you're already using this trick every day without realizing it.
JavaScript Core (17): Loose Equality, Strict Equality, and Implicit Coercion

Strictly speaking, JavaScript is kind of a nosy language — it loves to convert your types for you whether you asked it to or not. This post walks through loose equality (==), strict equality (===), and the implicit coercion that happens behind the scenes, with the MDN conversion table to back it up.
JavaScript Core Concepts (16) — Operators, Types & Grammar: Precedence and Associativity

We covered operators last time, so now let's talk about two things that trip people up: precedence and associativity. We'll walk through how JavaScript actually evaluates an arithmetic expression, why 1 < 2 < 3 and 3 > 2 > 1 give surprising results, and the sneaky expression behavior behind chained assignments like a = b = 1.
JavaScript Core Concepts (15): Operators Are Basically Tiny Functions

Next up: operators. You use them constantly no matter what you're building. This post covers what an operator actually is, why every operator is really just a tiny function under the hood, the operand counts (unary, binary, ternary), and what infix, prefix, and postfix expressions mean.
